rationale: Both candidates independently flag the trust_remote_code default flip (True→False), matching the author's self-acknowledged bug. Y goes further: it explicitly verifies and confirms the ground truth's central concern (endpoint restrictions resolved from the wrong/auto-detected pipeline vs. the final deploy-override pipeline), and separately surfaces the functools.cache staleness/test-pollution issue that parallels the reviewer's hf_config-mutability concern, plus a plausible reliability bug (UNSUPPORTED_ROUTES silently skipped in the fallback branch). X's remaining findings (frozenset/set type-annotation mismatch, missing docstrings, 'temporary' comment wording) are grounded but low-signal nitpicks that add noise rather than real coverage of the human reviewers' concerns. Neither candidate includes a latent-gap-check section. Both are concretely actionable with file/line citations, but Y's findings are more substantive and better matched to what the actual reviewers cared about.
